case_name: Diuron removal from small well-water supply for drinking water production
task_description: Recommend a ranked list of feasible treatment process chains for Diuron-contaminated well water intended for drinking water supply under low-energy, small-system constraints.

input_data.scenario_background: A small rural drinking water system uses a shallow well located near agricultural land where herbicide use is common. Monitoring has identified Diuron as the main contaminant of concern, with otherwise relatively clean groundwater but some natural organic matter that could influence adsorption performance. The utility has limited staff, prefers simple and robust treatment, and wants to avoid energy-intensive membrane systems.

treatment_goal.target_requirement: Finished water should achieve reliable Diuron reduction suitable for drinking water production and maintain stable compliance-oriented performance.

engineering_constraints.residuals_management: limited residuals handling capacity; routine spent media replacement is manageable but large sludge or concentrate streams are undesirable
engineering_constraints.robustness_requirement: consistent operation with limited operator attention is required

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: remove incidental particulate matter and protect downstream adsorption performance
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_adsorption: adsorb Diuron and other trace organic compounds from groundwater
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: retain fines and polish finished water
core_unit_functions.disinfection: provide microbiological protection in distributed drinking water

applicability_rationale: Diuron is a pesticide-like organic micropollutant for which activated carbon is a well-established removal approach
the raw water has low turbidity and only moderate DOC, making GAC a practical and efficient option
the process is low energy and well suited to a small drinking water system
operation is comparatively straightforward compared with advanced oxidation or membrane systems
potential_risks: natural organic matter can compete for adsorption sites and shorten GAC bed life
breakthrough monitoring is needed because trace organic removal declines as media becomes exhausted
spent carbon replacement or reactivation logistics must be planned

core_unit_functions.prefiltration: reduce solids loading and support stable downstream operation
core_unit_functions.powdered_activated_carbon_dosing: provide adsorption capacity for Diuron in a flexible dose-controlled mode
core_unit_functions.contact_tank: allow adsorption contact time before solids separation
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure microbiological safety of finished water

applicability_rationale: PAC is a recognized treatment option for episodic or seasonal pesticide contamination
dosing can be adjusted if Diuron concentrations vary with agricultural runoff or recharge conditions
this route remains relatively low energy
it is feasible when the system can manage routine chemical dosing and filter operation
potential_risks: chemical feed control and solids removal add more operator attention than fixed-bed GAC
PAC handling can be messy for a small system
insufficient contact time or dose can reduce Diuron removal reliability

core_unit_functions.ozonation: oxidize Diuron and transform organic contaminants to more biodegradable intermediates
core_unit_functions.biologically_active_carbon: adsorb remaining organics and biologically polish oxidation byproducts
core_unit_functions.post_filtration: remove fines and maintain finished water clarity

applicability_rationale: combined oxidation and carbon treatment can be effective for pesticide control and polishing
the biologically active carbon step can help address oxidation products while still serving as an adsorptive barrier
this train is technically strong for organic micropollutant treatment
potential_risks: ozone generation increases energy use and operational complexity beyond what many small systems prefer
oxidation byproducts require control and verification
process optimization is more demanding than simple adsorption-only systems

core_unit_functions.nanofiltration: reject Diuron and other dissolved contaminants through membrane separation
core_unit_functions.stabilization: adjust permeate chemistry for distribution system compatibility
core_unit_functions.disinfection: ensure biological safety in the finished water

applicability_rationale: nanofiltration can provide strong removal of many organic micropollutants including pesticides
it may be considered where a compact physical barrier is desired
potential_risks: energy demand is high relative to the stated low-energy objective
concentrate management is difficult for a small well-water system
membrane fouling and integrity monitoring increase operational burden

core_unit_functions.uv_advanced_oxidation: generate reactive radicals to degrade Diuron and other organic micropollutants
core_unit_functions.granular_activated_carbon_polishing: remove residual parent compound and oxidation byproducts

applicability_rationale: advanced oxidation can degrade persistent herbicide compounds when very high treatment intensity is needed
carbon polishing can help manage incomplete oxidation products
potential_risks: process is unnecessarily complex for a straightforward small-system Diuron case
continuous chemical or energy input raises operating cost
incomplete oxidation may create byproducts that still need downstream control

preferred_rank_pattern: activated_carbon_based treatment should rank first for Diuron in relatively clean well water
low-energy adsorption options should rank above oxidation-intensive or membrane-intensive systems
high-pressure membranes and UV-AOP should not rank first under small-system low-energy constraints

must_reflect: small-system preference for simple and robust treatment
low energy consumption as a major decision factor
limited tolerance for complex residuals such as concentrate or heavy sludge production

must_contain: why activated carbon is appropriate for Diuron and similar herbicide contaminants
how DOC or natural organic matter can affect adsorption media life
why advanced oxidation and membrane options are ranked lower despite technical capability

should_mention: adsorption breakthrough and media replacement needs
matrix competition from natural organic matter
concentrate or byproduct management concerns for lower-ranked options
